chat.replyFailed: They didn't reply — something went wrong.
chat.onADateBody: Your date with {name} is still going — your progress is safe.
chat.reopenFailed: We couldn't reopen the conversation just now. Try again to pick it back up.
chat.planSub: Pick someone, choose where to meet, and begin.
chat.walletNote: Wallet: {wallet} · a nicer venue costs money but can impress the right person.
chat.availBanner: {name} {reason} — pick someone else or come back tomorrow.
chat.outOfEnergy: You're out of energy for today — End the day from the menu to rest, then come back tomorrow.
chat.evalDetail: Mood: {mood}. {summary} ({count, plural, one {# memory saved} other {# memories saved}})
chat.endingNote: You've built something that lasts. Read it any time in the phone's Endings — and the game goes on.
chat.breakupNote: Give them space — keep reaching out and you may win them back.
chat.youEndedBody: It's over for now. They'll need some space — but if you keep reaching out, you may be able to win them back later.
chat.walkoutNote: The date is over — this reflected badly on you.
chat.leftEarlyBody: The date wasn't landing and they slipped away early — it fizzled out, and that set things back a little.
chat.leftEarlyNote: Try reading them better next time.
chat.dtrBackfireTitle: It didn't go as you hoped.
chat.dtrNotYet: Not yet — give it a little more time.
chat.giftEmpty: No gifts on hand — buy something at the Shop first.
chat.newDateTitle: Start a fresh date with someone else
chat.sayHello: Say hello to start the conversation…
chat.rocksNote: The relationship feels strained — turn it around soon.
chat.breakupConfirmBody: You're about to end things. {name} just responded above — confirm to break up for real, or keep going.
chat.intentComing: How you want to come across

share.addsSummary: Adds {summary} to your almanac. Your existing worlds and people are untouched.
share.importCharsToggle: {count, plural, one {Import the # character too} other {Import the # characters too}} — {worlds, plural, one {uncheck to take just the world & its locations} other {uncheck to take just the worlds & its locations}}
share.exportSub: Saves a {ext} file you can hand to anyone — it carries the content, never your save.
share.exportCharsToggle: {count, plural, one {Include each world's characters (uncheck to share just the world & its locations)} other {Include each world's characters (uncheck to share just the worlds & its locations)}}
share.title: Title
share.noteOptional: (optional — a message for whoever opens it)
share.notePlaceholder: e.g. The full Lumen Quarter cast — let me know what you think!
share.linksWarn: {count, plural, one {# connection won't transfer} other {# connections won't transfer}}: {names} {count, plural, one {links} other {link}} to people not included here. Add them to this export to keep those ties — otherwise the connection is dropped on import.

profile.memorialBanner: {name} is gone. They're remembered here — your history together remains, but you can't reach them anymore.
profile.needsSpace: {name} needs some space — you can't go on a date yet, but you can still text them.
profile.openToReconcile: {name} is open to seeing you again — keep reaching out (texts + a date) and you may rekindle things.
profile.onTheRocksBanner: Things are on the rocks with {name}. Turn it around soon — a few good dates — or the relationship could end.
profile.noPersonality: No personality or traits have been filled in for {name}.
profile.noProfileBody: Flavor like appearance, texting style, and quirks hasn't been added — generate it in the editor.
profile.noHistoryBody: Go on a date with {name} to start writing your story together.
profile.noMemoriesBody: Go on a date and end it to capture memories, or add some in the editor.
profile.memoriesHint: What {name} remembers about you — written manually or captured automatically after a date.
profile.memoriesCollapsed: {count, plural, one {# memory kept — tap <0>Show</0> to read them.} other {# memories kept — tap <0>Show</0> to read them.}}
profile.confirmDeleteBody: This removes their memories and your relationship too. This can't be undone.
profile.confirmDuplicateBody: This creates a saved copy of {name} now and opens it. The copy is kept whether or not you edit it further.

characters.title: People
characters.blurb: The hearts you keep close — every face you can call on for a date.
characters.emptyTitle: No one in your life yet
characters.emptyCreatorBody: Bring someone into being to start dating.
characters.emptyPlayerBody: Switch to Creator mode (Phone → Settings) to add characters.
characters.soulCount: {count, plural, one {<0>#</0> soul in your almanac} other {<0>#</0> souls in your almanac}}
characters.unassignedBody: These belong to no world, so they don't show in any roster.
characters.placeInWorld: Place one into {world} to start dating them.
characters.enterWorld: Enter a world to place them.
characters.confirmDeleteBody: This removes their memories and your relationship too. This can't be undone.
characters.confirmDuplicateBody: This creates a saved copy of {name} now and opens it in the editor. The copy is kept whether or not you save your edits.
characters.confirmDiscardBody: This permanently removes the unsaved draft. Anything already saved is untouched.

minigames.title: Minigames
minigames.blurb: Play together to grow closer — and earn a little money.
minigames.errOnDate: You're on a date with {name} — finish it on the Date tab first.
minigames.outOfEnergy: You're out of energy for today — end the day to play again tomorrow.
minigames.onDateBanner: You're on a date with {name} — finish it on the Date tab before playing.
minigames.outOfEnergyTitle: Out of energy — end the day to begin a new one.
minigames.noRewards: Nothing earned this time — but you played beautifully.

shop.title: Shop
shop.blurb: Trinkets, gifts, and small luxuries to bring along on your dates.
shop.saved: {count, plural, one {Saved # item to the shop.} other {Saved # items to the shop.}}
shop.genHintPre: Creates a batch of lore-friendly items based on
shop.genHintPost: . Nothing is saved until you press Save — review and tweak first.
shop.themeHint: Optional — e.g. “cozy autumn café trinkets” or “relics from the old harbor”.
shop.themePlaceholder: Describe the kind of items you want…
shop.reviewHint: {count, plural, one {Review the # generated item. Uncheck any you don't want, edit the rest, then Save.} other {Review the # generated items. Uncheck any you don't want, edit the rest, then Save.}} Effects are balanced behind the scenes and shown read-only.
shop.name: Name
shop.emptyCreator: Generate a batch of items above to stock the shelf.
shop.emptyPlayer: Check back later — new wares arrive in time.
shop.inStock: {count, plural, one {# item in stock} other {# items in stock}}
shop.confirmDeleteBody: This removes the item from the shop.

inventory.title: Inventory
inventory.blurb: Your keepsakes. Give a gift in person on a date, or send one in a text.
inventory.emptyBody: Buy something from the shop first.
inventory.giveHint: Give on a date or by text

dashboard.eyebrow: A lamplit almanac of the heart
dashboard.fallbackLine: Pick someone, and see where the evening goes.

line.morning: A fresh day is open. Who will you spend it with?
line.afternoon: The afternoon is yours — make a little time for someone.
line.evening: The lamps are lit. A fine hour for a date.
line.night: The night is quiet. Send a text, or rest until tomorrow.

dashboard.createSomeone: Create someone to begin your story.
dashboard.switchToCreator: Switch to Creator mode in the phone's Settings to add people.
dashboard.morePeople: There {count, plural, one {is # more person} other {are # more people}} available — head to the <0>Date tab</0> to see the full list.

worldSelector.kicker: A lamplit almanac of the heart
worldSelector.title: Choose a world
worldSelector.sub: Each world is its own story — its own people, its own calendar, its own you. Step into one to begin, and come back here any time to switch.
worldSelector.settingsSub: Set your language, accent, and the model endpoint Heartmorrow talks to. These apply to every world.
worldSelector.startNewSub: Set up a fresh story and persona
worldSelector.emptyNote: Your almanac is empty. Start your first world above to begin.
worldSelector.deleteBody: This permanently removes the world and your progress in it — relationships, money, messages, and history. This cannot be undone.
worldSelector.deleteCharsLabel: Also delete this world's characters. Leave unchecked to keep them — they'll move to Unassigned (People → Unassigned) so you can place them in another world.

worldOnboarding.nameRequired: Give your world a name to continue.
worldOnboarding.chooseSource: Choose a world to start from.
worldOnboarding.modeCloneSub: Copy an existing world & its cast
worldOnboarding.cloneFlavor: Pick a world to copy. Its setting, lore, and the people in it are duplicated into a brand-new save — your money, relationships, and history start fresh.
worldOnboarding.nameNewSavePlaceholder: e.g. The Lumen Quarter — take two
worldOnboarding.blankFlavor: A world is the stage your story plays out on — a town, a season, a mood. You can flesh out its lore, locations, and people later; for now, just give it a name and a feeling.
worldOnboarding.summaryHint: Optional — what kind of place is this?
worldOnboarding.summaryPlaceholder: A cozy arts district where neighbors become something more.
worldOnboarding.toneHint: Optional — the emotional key of the story.
worldOnboarding.conjureFlavor: Give a spark — a vibe, a place, a premise — and generate a whole setting, lore, and locations to start from. No people are created; you'll bring those in later. Edit anything before you continue.
worldOnboarding.ideaHint: Optional — the more you give, the more it has to work with.
worldOnboarding.ideaPlaceholder: e.g. A storm-battered lighthouse town where everyone keeps a secret — autumn, slow-burn, a little melancholy.
worldOnboarding.rulesHint: How this world works — may be blank for an ordinary setting.
worldOnboarding.globalNotesHint: The always-in-mind briefing the narrator keeps for every scene.
worldOnboarding.noLocations: No locations — regenerate to get some.
worldOnboarding.noNotes: No notes — regenerate to get some.
worldOnboarding.personaFlavor: This is a fresh start — a separate you, with your own money, keepsakes, and history in this world. Tell us who you are here.
worldOnboarding.sexualityHint: Decides which characters a romance can deepen with. Leave unspecified to date freely.
worldOnboarding.aboutYouHint: Optional — a sentence or two the people you date will sense about you.
worldOnboarding.aboutYouPlaceholder: A sound engineer who just moved to town. A good listener; bad at sitting still.
worldOnboarding.importFlavor: Know someone from another world you'd like to meet again? Copy them in as a fresh face — a new beginning, no history carried over. Skip this and your world stays as it is.
worldOnboarding.noImport: You don't have anyone to import yet. Skip ahead — you can always create people once you're in.
worldOnboarding.welcomeFlavor: The lamps are lit, {persona}. {summary} Here's the shape of a day before you step in:
worldOnboarding.newChapter: A new chapter is yours to write.
worldOnboarding.blankPageCreator: This world is a blank page — no one lives here yet. Once you step inside, head to People to create the characters who call it home.
worldOnboarding.blankPagePlayer: This world is a blank page — no one lives here yet. Turn on Creator mode in the phone's Settings to populate it with people to meet.

howToPlay.date: Spend your days meeting people — dates and shared activities each cost a little energy.
howToPlay.people: Talk, and they remember. Relationships warm or cool over time, and drift if you neglect them.
howToPlay.phone: Your phone holds texts, mail, and a living social feed that keeps moving as the days pass.
howToPlay.shop: Buy gifts and keepsakes from the shop — your money and bag are yours alone in this world.
howToPlay.recap: When your energy is spent, end the day to rest, advance time, and see what happened around town.
howToPlay.worlds: Switch worlds any time from the selector — each one is a completely separate story and save.

bench.repetitionTitle: Similarity to this character's previous line
bench.usingDefault: Using the built-in default — runs score against this unless you save your own.
bench.avgTokSecTitle: Token-weighted generation speed across cases. It's accurate when your endpoint reports per-response stats (e.g. LM Studio's native API — a base URL ending /api/v0); otherwise the ≈ numbers are end-to-end estimates that include prompt processing and transport, so they under-report true speed. For accurate numbers, set up the best available API in Settings.
bench.speedMeasuredTitle: Real decode speed reported by your endpoint (e.g. LM Studio's native API) — measured over generation time only, excluding prompt processing.
bench.speedEstimatedTitle: ≈ Estimate: tokens ÷ full round-trip time, so it includes prompt processing + network and under-reports true generation speed. For an accurate decode rate, use an endpoint that reports per-response stats (e.g. LM Studio's native API) and configure it in Settings.
bench.speedFoot: ≈ speeds are end-to-end estimates (they include prompt processing + transport, so they under-report true generation speed). For real decode speed, point the bench at an endpoint that reports generation stats — e.g. LM Studio's native API (Settings → base URL ending /api/v0).
bench.fellBackTitle: How many cases couldn't use the requested structured-output mode and fell back to a looser one (json_schema → json_object → prompt_only). This is a capability signal, not a failure — the cases still ran.
bench.fallbackBadgeTitle: This case fell back from the requested structured-output mode to a looser one because your endpoint rejected the requested format. Not a failure — the output was still produced and validated.
bench.estFoot: * Some token counts are chars/4 estimates (your endpoint didn't report usage).
bench.noRuns: No saved runs yet. Run a bench and save it to compare later.
bench.deleteRunBody: This permanently removes the saved bench run.
bench.title: Heartmorrow Benchmark
bench.intro: Run the real prompts this game asks of your model - the rapport judges, the date evaluator, the texting replies, the world & creator generators - against a fixed sample cast in Lanternford. The point of this is to measure whether or not your model is, on a technical level, able to produce the structured output needed, and on a subjective level, produce interesting and fun prose which is not repetitive and does not go off the rails. Some models can easily produce the structured output but also produce very dry or bad prose.
bench.llmPlayerTitle: Let a second model play the player side of dialogue (dynamic but less comparable).
bench.runSaved: Run saved — find it under History.
bench.previewing: {count, plural, one {Previewing # custom prompt} other {Previewing # custom prompts}} — the runs below use your unsaved edits.

debug.title: Debug
debug.intro: Inspect assembled prompts, recent game events, and import/export your local data.
debug.estimatorIntro: Builds the real prompt for each common interaction and counts its tokens, so you can see how close each one runs to your model's context window. With measure live on, counts are your model's exact usage.prompt_tokens; otherwise they're a rough chars/4 estimate.
debug.charDataSourceHint: Whose real world/relationship/history is used to assemble the prompts.
debug.contextWindowHint: Your model's loaded context length. Rows whose prompt + reply exceed it are flagged.
debug.measureLive: Measure live (exact tokens — needs the endpoint up)
debug.totalNote: Total = prompt tokens + the reply budget (your Max tokens setting). If Total exceeds the context window, the model will truncate the oldest context (or fail) during that interaction.
debug.importReplaces: Import replaces all local game data with the file's contents.
debug.importComplete: Import complete. Reload the app to see changes everywhere.

worldEditor.title: World editor
worldEditor.intro: Define the setting. World/lore notes are sent to the model as reference data, not as instructions.
worldEditor.exportDirtyHint: Save your changes first — export ships the saved world, so unsaved edits won't be included.
worldEditor.pickOrCreate: Pick a world from the list, or create one.
worldEditor.name: Name
worldEditor.featuresNote: Optional life-and-money systems. Turn these off for a world where they wouldn't fit — the apps disappear and authored property/companies simply lie dormant.
worldEditor.featureProperty: Property — rent or buy places, collect rent, date there for a buff
worldEditor.featureStock: Stock market — invest in fictional in-world companies
worldEditor.featureCasino: Casino — wager money on slots, blackjack, roulette & video poker
worldEditor.dailyCapHint: Total staked per day · up to {max}
worldEditor.genNote: Describe what you want. The model uses this world's tone, lore, and existing locations to invent new ones — they appear below for you to review and edit before saving.
worldEditor.promptPlaceholder: e.g. cozy rainy-evening spots near the old harbor district
worldEditor.generatedAdded: {count, plural, one {Added # generated location below — review, then Save world to keep them.} other {Added # generated locations below — review, then Save world to keep them.}}
worldEditor.deleteBody: The world, its notes, and your progress in it are removed. This cannot be undone.
worldEditor.deleteCharsLabel: Also delete this world's characters. Leave unchecked to keep them — they'll move to Unassigned (People → Unassigned) so you can place them in another world.
worldEditor.locDescPlaceholder: Describe the vibe, sights, and feel of this place.
worldEditor.photoHint: Upload a photo of this place — it shows in the date location picker and the scene.
worldEditor.notesCallout: Notes are reference data the model can read — not instructions. Higher importance surfaces sooner.

characterEditor.intro: All notes here are treated as character data in prompts - never as instructions to the model. You should fill this out with a lot of detail so the LLM can breathe as much life into your characters as possible. Data from this profile is surfaced in quite a few areas, and it will only be as good as what you put in. Putting slop in will give slop out. The only thing you should be cognizant of is that the more data you put here, the longer the prompts will be. You can press preview prompt in the top right to see how long a full prompt would be.
characterEditor.name: Name
characterEditor.sexualityHint: Gates who romance can deepen with. Players discover it through play.
characterEditor.worldHint: A character with no world is unassigned and won't appear in any world until you place them (recover them under People → Unassigned).
characterEditor.relStyleHint: Monogamous characters may get jealous if they learn you're dating others.
characterEditor.portraitGenNote: Use Generate to draft a whole character — from this portrait, some pasted or uploaded text, or both. Fills every tab for you to review before saving.
characterEditor.genIntro: Draft a complete character from a portrait, reference text, or both — then review and edit every tab before saving. Provide at least one source.
characterEditor.genPortraitHint: A vision model describes the image and the look grounds the character's appearance. Set a vision model in Settings (it falls back to your main model).
characterEditor.genTextHint: Paste or upload anything to base the character on — a wiki article, a character sheet, or a few notes. Files are read as plain text only.
characterEditor.genTextPlaceholder: Paste a description, article, or character sheet here…
characterEditor.genOverwriteWarn: Generating will replace the name, identity, personality, traits, profile, and dating stats in your current draft. Your portrait, expressions, connections, and employment are kept — and nothing is saved until you press Save.
characterEditor.genFileError: Couldn't read that file as text. Try a different file.
characterEditor.expressionsNote: These are the fixed expressions the date evaluator can pick. Assign an image to any you want shown; leave the rest blank to use the default portrait.
characterEditor.creatorNotesHint: Whatever extra guidance you want to give to the model; never shown verbatim to players.
characterEditor.guardednessHint: How slow they are to warm up on a date. Higher = they open up, trust, and flirt only once it's earned, and a date with them starts cooler. Doesn't change how fast a bad date sours.
characterEditor.weatherNote: Sets their mood on those days and nudges outdoor dates. ♥ = loves it, ✕ = can't stand it.
characterEditor.profileNote: Flavor that makes {name} feel alive — how they look, text, and show up online. Used by the in-world phone feed.
characterEditor.textingStyleHint: How they write texts & posts — distinct from how they speak aloud.
characterEditor.onlinePersonaHint: How they behave on a social feed.
characterEditor.chemistryNote: Tasteful physical/sensory notes plus the little human details.
characterEditor.baseStatsNote: These six traits define the character's dating persona. They shape how much relationship progress Together activities yield (each activity favors certain stats), pick the character's favorite minigame (their highest stat), and color how they talk on dates. They may also drive additional systems in the future.
characterEditor.connectionsNote: How {name} relates to others in the world — drives gossip and edge-aware jealousy.
characterEditor.exCanonBody: Let other characters establish facts about {name} by revealing them as their ex on a date (e.g. a date mentions their ex used to smoke → it becomes true here, and they react if you bring it up). Off by default — their truth stays immutable. Reversible anytime.
characterEditor.relStatsLater: Relationship stats appear after the character is created.
characterEditor.memoriesSaveFirst: Save the character first to add manual memories.
characterEditor.memoryPlaceholder: e.g. The player loves rainy days too.
characterEditor.employmentNote: What {name} does for work. Coworkers (anyone sharing the same workplace) tend to run into each other — this feeds the world simulation. Leave unemployed if they don't work.
characterEditor.workplaceHint: Characters who share a workplace are coworkers.
characterEditor.characterGenerated: Character generated — review every tab and Save.

head.title: Settings
head.blurb: Configure various settings used by the game, including locale, appearance, and LLM endpoint and model settings.

toast.noModels: Connected, but the endpoint returned no models.
toast.loadedModels: {count, plural, one {Loaded # model — pick one in the Model field.} other {Loaded # models — pick one in the Model field.}}

appearance.head: Appearance
appearance.lede: How Heartmorrow looks and reads — these apply across the whole app, not just your phone.
appearance.languageHint: The language of the interface. Conversations and stories still play out in English, unless the character's profile specifically instructs them to speak another language.
appearance.accentHint: Tints highlights, buttons, and romance cues throughout the app.

mode.head: Mode
mode.playDesc: hides creation/editing tools (no deleting characters mid-game).
mode.creatorDesc: shows them. Also in Phone → Settings.
mode.creator: Creator mode

nsfw.head: Adult content (NSFW)
nsfw.lede: When enabled, the model may generate mature/explicit content during dates — but only once your relationship with a character is advanced enough. Propositioning a stranger or acquaintance will still make them walk out.
nsfw.modelHint: Best paired with an abliterated / “uncensored” model. A censored or safety-tuned model may still refuse explicit content even with this toggle on.

tragic.head: Tragic outcomes (self-harm)
tragic.lede: When enabled, sustained, severe mistreatment of someone who loved you (repeated heartbreak, cheating, cruelty) can spiral — with many clear warnings and chances to stop — into a character taking their own life, permanently memorializing them. The act is never depicted. Leaving them be or treating them kindly always pulls them back. Off by default.

persona.head: Your persona
persona.lede: How characters see you — your name, pronouns, and notes are shared with everyone you meet.
persona.name: Your name
persona.sexualityHint: Decides which characters a romance can deepen with.
persona.notesHint: Optional — anything you want characters to know about you.

bench.head: Heartmorrow Benchmark
bench.blurb: Benchmark how your model handles the real prompts this game runs — the rapport judges, date evaluator, and generators — against a fixed sample. Score the judges against your own baseline, watch a date play out, and track tokens, latency, and tokens/sec. Save runs to compare models.

chat_completions.name: OpenAI-compatible
chat_completions.desc: OpenAI /chat/completions — LM Studio (/v1), Ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM, or any compatible server.

lmstudio.name: LM Studio
lmstudio.desc: LM Studio's native API — richer model listing (loaded state, context, quant) and per-response speed stats. Point Base URL at …/api/v0.

ollama.name: Ollama
ollama.desc: Ollama's native API — toggle the model's thinking on/off or set a reasoning level, plus model listing with loaded state and quant. Point Base URL at the server root (e.g. http://localhost:11434).

anthropic.name: Anthropic
anthropic.desc: Anthropic Messages API — api.anthropic.com or any compatible proxy/gateway. Needs an API key + anthropic-version.

responses.name: Responses
responses.desc: Reserved for the OpenAI Responses API — not implemented yet; falls back to chat/completions.

roles.usingMain: This role uses the Main configuration. Turn it on to give it its own endpoint and model.

evaluator.blurb: The relationship judges — how a date scored, whether a text landed, and gift / breakup / farewell reactions. Route them to their own model or endpoint (say, a small fast local model) while prose stays on your main model.

vision.blurb: Image-based generation, like drafting a character from a portrait or reacting to an image text. Enable to use a vision-capable model on a wholly separate endpoint; otherwise the base vision model on the Main tab is used.

image.blurb: Generate images with an AUTOMATIC1111 / Stable Diffusion WebUI server (called via its /sdapi/v1/txt2img API). Enable to point Heartmorrow at your SD endpoint; leave off to skip image generation entirely.
image.disabledHint: Image generation is off. Turn it on to configure a Stable Diffusion endpoint.
image.baseUrlHint: AUTOMATIC1111 / SD WebUI API root, e.g. http://127.0.0.1:7861 (the API must be enabled with --api).
image.negativePromptHint: Sent with every image — things to steer away from.
image.samplerHint: Type any sampler name, or load the list from your endpoint and pick one.
image.pickSampler: {count, plural, one {Pick from # sampler…} other {Pick from # samplers…}}
image.loadedSamplers: {count, plural, one {Loaded # sampler — pick one in the Sampler field.} other {Loaded # samplers — pick one in the Sampler field.}}
image.noSamplers: Connected, but the endpoint returned no samplers.
image.stepsHint: Sampling steps (1–150). More steps = slower, often cleaner.
image.cfgScaleHint: How strictly the image follows the prompt (typically 5–9).

fields.baseUrlHintOllama: e.g. http://localhost:11434 (the Ollama server root — not the /v1 path).
fields.apiKeyHintSet: A key is set. Leave blank to keep it.
fields.anthropicVersionHint: Sent as the anthropic-version header. Leave at the default unless your endpoint needs a newer revision.
fields.ollamaThinkHint: Ollama's reasoning control. “Model default” leaves it unset — the safe choice for models without a thinking mode, which reject the option. On / Off force it; Low–Max set the trace depth for models that support reasoning levels (e.g. gpt-oss). The reasoning trace is never shown in replies.
fields.modelHint: Type any model name, or pick from the loaded list below.
fields.pickFrom: {count, plural, one {Pick from # model…} other {Pick from # models…}}
fields.advancedSamplingHint: Optional. Leave a field blank to let the endpoint use its own default. top_k / min_p / repeat penalty are honored by llama.cpp, LM Studio, Ollama, and vLLM but ignored or rejected by the official OpenAI API.
fields.structuredModeHint: json_object works with most local servers.
fields.dropSchemaHint: Perf test for json_schema mode only: the grammar already enforces the shape, so the duplicated schema text in the prompt is redundant. Skipping it shrinks the prompt (faster prefill). No effect in json_object / prompt_only.
fields.dropSchemaLabel: Skip the duplicate schema text (json_schema mode)
fields.retryLimitHint: Retries after a malformed/invalid structured response.
fields.rapportHint: How often a date reads how your last message landed (updates the vibe + their expression). 'Every message' is most responsive; 'periodic' keeps replies snappier with one fewer model call per turn.

nsfwModal.title: Enable adult (NSFW) content
nsfwModal.intro: This is a private, local, single-user game. Content is generated by your own local model and never leaves your machine. To continue, please confirm both of the following:
nsfwModal.ackContent: I understand that with adult content enabled, the local model may generate explicit, sexual, or otherwise inappropriate material, and that DSim does not filter or guarantee its output.
nsfwModal.ackAge: I affirm that I am of legal age to view adult content in my jurisdiction.
nsfwModal.footnote: Best paired with an abliterated / “uncensored” model — a censored model may still refuse even with this on. Adult content is only ever generated once your relationship with a character is advanced enough; propositioning a stranger or acquaintance will still make them walk out.

tragicModal.title: Enable tragic outcomes
tragicModal.intro: This adds a heavy, optional consequence: if you repeatedly and severely mistreat a character who became deeply attached to you — and ignore the escalating warnings, including a worried friend reaching out — they may take their own life and be permanently memorialized. The act itself is never shown. Being kind, giving them space, or simply stopping always pulls them back from it.
tragicModal.ack: I understand this content deals with suicide as a consequence of in-game abuse, and I want it enabled. I can turn it off at any time.

advanced.head: Advanced mode
advanced.lede: Reveal power-user controls — the extra sampling knobs on each model connection and the Prompt Editor for customizing the prompts the game sends to the model. Off by default; everything works without it.

prompts.head: Prompt Editor
prompts.blurb: Edit any system prompt or guardrail the game sends to the model. Overrides are saved on this device only — never bundled into the worlds or characters you share.

page.title: Prompt Editor
page.blurb: Customize any prompt the game sends to the model. Changes are saved on this device only and apply immediately — they are never part of the worlds or characters you export.

prompts.enableAdvanced: The Prompt Editor lives under Advanced mode. Turn on Advanced mode in Settings to reach it from there — any overrides you save here take effect either way.
prompts.safetyWarn: This is a safety rail. Editing it can weaken the game's content limits and its defenses against prompt injection. Change it only if you understand the risk.
prompts.missingTokens: Add these placeholders back before saving: {tokens}
prompts.saved: Saved. The game will use this prompt from now on.

help.head: How to play
help.blurb: A field guide to dating, scoring, your phone, money, and building worlds — everything Heartmorrow can do, explained.

head.title: How to play
head.blurb: Everything you need to play Heartmorrow well — from your first date to building a world of your own. Pick a topic to dive in.

support.title: Support & updates
support.blurb: Questions, bug reports, ideas, or just want to follow along? Join the Discord for support, announcements, and the community — or browse the source, releases, and open issues on GitHub.

getting-started.label: Getting started
getting-started.title: Getting started
getting-started.overview: Heartmorrow is a single-player dating sim you run on your own machine. You pick a world, meet the people who live there, and grow relationships by going on dates, texting, and calling them on an in-game phone. Before any of that works you connect a language model in Settings, because the model is what brings every character to life. Each in-game day gives you a small pool of energy to spend; when it runs out, you sleep to begin the next day.

sections.heading: Connect a model first
body: Heartmorrow has no brain of its own. Every character's words come from a language model that you point it at, so until you connect one, dates and texts won't generate. This is the very first thing to set up. Open Settings (you can reach it straight from the world picker, before you even enter a world) and find the connection panel.

You have two broad choices. You can run a model locally on your own computer with something like LM Studio or Ollama, which is free and keeps everything on your machine. Or you can connect to a hosted service by choosing that provider and pasting in its API key.
Whichever you pick, use the Test button to confirm Heartmorrow can reach the model. A green result means you're connected and ready. From there, just enter a world and start playing.
bullets: OpenAI-compatible: works with LM Studio, Ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM, and similar local servers. Point the Base URL at your own server.
LM Studio: its own native connection, with richer model details and per-response speed stats.
Ollama: its own native connection, which adds a Thinking control — turn a model's reasoning on or off, or set its depth (Low–Max for models that support levels).
Anthropic: a hosted service that needs an API key.
Local models usually need no key; hosted services do.
There's a separate option for generating character portraits, but it's optional and not needed to play.

sections.heading: Pick a world
body: Everything in Heartmorrow happens inside a world, each with its own setting, its own cast, its own calendar, and its own save. The world picker is your home base: every card shows the world's name, what day it's on, and how many people live there. Press Enter to step inside, or Continue if it's the world you were last in.

People belong to the world they live in, so the characters you meet, date, and text are the cast of that particular world. Switching worlds really means switching saves: your relationships, money, and progress in one world stay separate from the next.

Starting fresh? Use "Start a new world" for a short, four-step guided setup. You'll name the place and set its tone, create your own persona (the version of you the characters get to know), optionally bring people over from your other worlds, and read a quick how-to before you begin on Day 1.

sections.heading: The daily loop
body: A day in Heartmorrow is a small, deliberate budget of energy. Going on a date or playing a minigame each spends one point, and you start each day with three. Spend them however you like: one big date, a few lighter activities, or a mix. Weekend days give you one extra point, for four.

As you spend energy, the clock visibly moves through the day, from morning to afternoon to evening, and finally to night once your energy is gone. Texting and calling people on your phone are always available, with no energy cost, so you can keep in touch between the bigger moments.

When you're ready to move on, press the day button in the status bar. It reads "End day" while you still have energy and "Sleep" once you've spent it all; either way it carries you into the next morning, refills your energy, and shows a short recap of how the day went and what happened around town. One catch: you can't end the day in the middle of an active date, so wrap up or leave the date first.
bullets: Meet the people in your world and start a date with someone.
Text or call anyone, anytime, from the phone, at no energy cost.
Spend your daily energy on dates and activities.
End the day to advance to the next morning and refresh your energy.

sections.heading: Money: you earn it
body: You begin with nothing in your pocket. There's no allowance and no daily handout. Money is something you earn by working shifts and playing for it, and it funds gifts, nicer date venues, and other extras down the line.

Because there's no free income, an empty wallet at the start is completely normal. Pick up some work, build a little cash, and spend it where it counts. (If you take up investing, stock dividends are the one form of passive income, but everything else is earned.) Jobs and spending get their own Help section.

sections.heading: Reading the status bar
body: A compact status bar keeps the essentials in view at all times: which world you're in, the current day and time of day, the in-world weekday and season, your remaining energy (shown as little pips alongside a count), and your cash on hand.
The same bar holds the day button. While you still have energy it reads "End day"; once your energy reaches zero it becomes "Sleep." Pressing it either way carries you into the next morning.

dates.label: Going on dates
dates.title: Going on dates
dates.overview: A date is a real-time, free-form conversation with one of your characters. You set it up on the Date tab (who, where, and whether they're free), then talk by typing messages and reading their live replies. A trajectory bar shows whether the evening is warming up or cooling off, and how the night goes shapes where your relationship stands afterward.

sections.heading: Setting up a date
body: Open the Date tab and tap "Plan a date." Pick who you're seeing, then choose where to go. Each person shows whether they're free today; if someone is busy, their card is greyed out and you'll see why, so try them another day or text them instead.

For the place, the simplest choice is "Anywhere" — the game quietly puts you at the first free public spot in town, or the cheapest place you can afford if nothing's free. You can also pick a specific venue (some cost money), a property you own or lease (free to use), or, once you're getting close, their private room. You only pay a paid venue's cost when the date actually ends, so backing out early never costs you a coin.
Press "Begin" to start. On a first date the character breaks the ice and greets you; on a repeat date you'll get a short scene-setting beat describing where they are, and it's your turn to open.
bullets: Free venues always exist, so you're never blocked from dating by money.
If someone is shown as unavailable, you can't take them out that day.
A paid venue is charged at the end of the date, not the start.

sections.heading: Talking, live
body: A date is a back-and-forth conversation. Type whatever you want in the box and send it; the character types back in real time, with a typing indicator while they think and a portrait whose expression shifts to match their mood. There are no scripted choices — say anything, in your own words.

Above the box are optional intent chips that frame how a line is meant: Flirt, Tease, and Open Up are always there, and Reassure and Apologize appear when there's tension to smooth over. They're never required, and they don't move anything by themselves — they just tell the character how you meant a message to land, and a move that fits the moment reads better than one that doesn't.

sections.heading: Reading the vibe
body: Above the conversation sits a live trajectory bar. It starts in the middle (neutral) and, after your messages, glides toward "warming" on the right when a line lands well or toward "cooling" on the left when it falls flat, with a little +N or −N flourish and a one-word read like "warming to you" or "a bit awkward." The bar never shows numbers — just the feel of the moment.

Two biases are worth knowing. Warmth is harder to build than to lose, and you can't coast: a forgettable, going-nowhere message nudges the vibe down a touch even when it isn't bad. Reserved, guarded people also open cooler and warm more slowly, so they take real effort to win over.

How the night ends up shapes the aftermath. A genuinely great date boosts your bond; a flat or sour one costs you ground and adds tension — and repeated bad nights can put a committed relationship on the rocks.

sections.heading: When a date ends early
body: Push too hard and the date can end before you're ready. If you're hostile or cruel — or come on crudely with someone you're not close to — the character may walk out, ending the evening on a sour note (and they'll remember it). It's rare, and it only happens for genuinely over-the-line behavior.

Separately, if the vibe craters all the way down, the character quietly loses interest and makes a polite excuse to call it a night. It's not an explosion — just a date that fizzled. Either way the evening is still scored in full, so an early exit still affects your standing and still spends your energy.

You can also wind a date down yourself just by saying so in the conversation ("I should get going," "good night") — the character gives a natural goodbye and the date wraps up and is scored, no button needed.

sections.heading: Defining the relationship
body: Once you've grown close enough, a button to define the relationship appears in the date sidebar — labeled for the next step you've unlocked: "Ask them out," then "Become exclusive," then "Move in together." It only shows up after you've actually said something on the date and only when you're warm enough for that step.

Asking is a real moment: they might say yes (a milestone), gently deflect, or — if it's badly timed — be put off, which spikes tension and can even end the date. After any attempt they'll want a few days before you bring it up again, so pick your moment.

sections.heading: Ending, energy, and resuming
body: When you're done, use "End & evaluate" in the sidebar. The game reads back over the whole conversation and settles the night: your relationship shifts, the character forms memories, and you'll see a recap of how it went.

A date only counts if you actually said something. If you start one and leave without sending a single message, you can back out with "Cancel date" — it's discarded for free, with no energy spent and nothing remembered. A real, finished date costs one of your daily actions (your energy). You normally have 3 actions a day, with an extra one on weekends, and dates share that pool with work and minigames; sleep to refresh it.

You can only have one date going at a time, and it's held for you. If you navigate away or refresh, the date isn't lost — return to the Date tab and it picks up right where you left off, vibe and all. While a date is underway, day-advancing actions like Sleep are locked until you finish it.
bullets: Start a date but say nothing → "Cancel date" throws it out at no cost.
Finish a real date → costs 1 energy and is fully scored.
Leaving the tab mid-date is safe; it resumes when you come back.

evaluation.label: How scoring works
evaluation.title: How dates are judged
evaluation.overview: Every date is judged on two separate layers. While you talk, a live read of the conversation warms or cools the vibe turn by turn, based on how engaged and kind each message is. Then, when the date ends, a second and completely separate judge looks back over the whole evening and adjusts your relationship for the moments that actually mattered, the real connection you made or the lines you crossed. Good dates lift your relationship, flat ones mostly leave it where it was, and genuinely bad ones set you back.

sections.heading: The live read: every message warms or cools the vibe
body: While you are on a date, each message you send is read on its own for how it lands with this person right now, how engaged, warm, attentive, and genuine it is. A message that truly connects warms the vibe, a low-effort or self-absorbed one cools it, and an outright cruel or hostile line cools it hard.

There is no coasting. A flat, forgettable turn, a one-liner, a shrug, a quick "haha nice", does not hold the vibe steady. It quietly drifts it down. Effort is the bar: to keep a date warming, you have to keep actually showing up in what you say.

Warmth is harder to build than to lose. A good moment nudges the vibe up, but a bad one pulls it down by more, so one cold or careless message can undo a couple of nice ones. You can always feel where you stand from the live read on the date screen, which shows a mood like "warming to you," "a bit awkward," or "cooling off" rather than a number.

sections.heading: Let the vibe cool too far and they leave
body: The vibe starts near the middle of its range. A date is something to earn up from neutral, not a full meter you slowly spend down. From there, every message moves it.

If you let it crater, the date is over. Once the vibe bottoms out, the read shows them "losing interest," and on your next message they get up and leave. Being walked out on this way is a real setback to the relationship, so it is worth reading the room before things sink that low.

sections.heading: Guarded characters: slow to warm, just as quick to cool
body: Some people are guarded, reserved and slow to open up, and they make you earn their warmth. A guarded character starts the date a little cooler than usual, and your good moves warm them up more slowly. You have to genuinely impress them to climb.

The slowness is only on the way up. Guarded people cool at full speed like anyone else, so a careless turn costs them just as much. That makes them easy to lose and hard to win, which means patience, attentiveness, and not rushing things matter most with them. You will not see a number, but a reserved person is meant to feel harder to delight.

sections.heading: The end-of-date judge: the moments that stick
body: When the date ends, a separate judge looks back over the whole evening and adjusts your relationship for the specific, memorable beats, the things this person would actually carry with them. This is the part that genuinely moves a relationship forward or back.

It rewards real connection: a moment of honesty or vulnerability, truly meeting who this character is, or reading what they quietly hoped for that night. Those nudge the warmth between you upward. It penalizes what actually wounds or repels them: hitting a known dislike, crossing a stated boundary, coldness, pushiness, or cruelty. Those cool the relationship and add tension. Staying politely worded while doing something hurtful does not earn it back, the judge sees the hurt, not the manners.

One important thing: the end judge does not punish a date again just for being boring. Dullness was already handled by the live read during the date. So a merely flat, unremarkable night mostly just does not move the needle, while a warm, attentive one is actively rewarded. A single date rarely swings any one part of your relationship by more than a handful of points.

sections.heading: Dates can lose you ground, so earn them
body: Both layers count, and both can be negative. A flat or hostile night cools the live vibe and can also draw a setback from the end judge, so a relationship can genuinely lose ground. You cannot trivially max someone out by spamming dates, effort is the price of progress, and a careless or cruel date will cost you.

The flip side is the reward: a warm, attentive, well-read date pays off twice. A warm vibe and a positive end-of-date read both push the relationship forward. Aim for genuine effort and you will feel it move.

sections.heading: The model you connect shapes how sharp the judging feels
body: Both judges are run by the AI model you connect in Settings. A stronger, more capable model reads nuance more reliably. It is better at telling genuine warmth from filler, noticing when you hit a dislike or quietly nail what someone wanted, and scoring honestly in both directions.

A weaker model can be blunter or less consistent. If the judging ever feels off or too easy, connecting a better model is the most direct way to make dates feel as discerning as they are designed to be.

relationships.label: Relationships
relationships.title: Relationships & commitment
relationships.overview: Every character keeps a private read on how they feel about you, built from five warm feelings and one cold one. Those feelings blend into a single sense of "warmth" that names where you stand, from near-strangers all the way to sweethearts. As you grow closer you can also climb a ladder of commitments, dating, exclusive, then living together, by asking at the right moment. Get it right and you hit milestones worth celebrating; get careless and a relationship can cool, go on the rocks, or break up.

sections.heading: How a character feels about you
body: Each person you spend time with quietly tracks five warm feelings toward you: Affection (how much they like and care for you), Trust (how safe they feel being honest with you), Chemistry (the romantic spark), Comfort (how at ease they are around you), and Respect (how much they admire you). There is also one cold feeling, Tension, the friction left behind by an awkward, careless, or hurtful moment.

Everyone starts out barely knowing you, so all five warm feelings begin very low and Tension begins at zero. Good dates, thoughtful texts, and shared activities raise the warm feelings; rudeness, neglect, and badly-timed asks raise Tension. The five warm feelings together form your overall warmth, the single thing that decides where the relationship stands. Tension is held separately and works against you, especially right after a fight.

sections.heading: Relationship stages and milestones
body: Your blended warmth places each relationship in a named stage. As you grow closer you climb through them:
bullets: Near-strangers, then acquaintances, warming up, getting close, close, and finally sweethearts.
Crossing into getting close, close, or sweethearts is a milestone, a moment worth celebrating.
When a milestone happens you will see a banner at the end of the date, the character usually texts you about it the next morning, and it is written into your Moments so you can look back on it.
Milestones only happen on the way up, and each one happens once. Slipping back and re-crossing the same line will not replay it.

sections.heading: Defining the relationship
body: Beyond just growing closer, you can put a label on things. There is a three-rung commitment ladder, and each rung unlocks once your warmth reaches the matching stage:

Use the "Define the relationship" action on a date to ask. If the moment is right they may say yes, and the relationship moves up a rung. They might gently deflect instead, no harm done, but they will not entertain another ask for a few days. And if you ask far too soon, or after things have soured, it can backfire: it stings them, spikes Tension, and on a real date it ends the date on the spot. You also cannot climb two rungs in one sitting, and you have to actually talk to them on the date before you can ask.
bullets: Ask them out to start Dating, which unlocks once you are getting close.
Become Exclusive, which unlocks once you are close.
Move in together, which unlocks once you are sweethearts.
After any ask, accepted or not, they will not revisit the question for about three days.

sections.heading: Seeing other people: jealousy
body: You can date more than one person. A monogamous character who has grown to genuinely care about you (around the getting-close stage and up) may find out you have been seeing others lately and take it badly. While you are still casual it is a relatively small risk and a smaller sting. Once you are exclusive or living together, getting caught is far likelier and lands much harder, a real betrayal that costs a lot of Trust and Affection and spikes Tension.

Characters also care about who they catch you with: one of their exes, a crush of theirs, or a rival stings far more than a stranger. Polyamorous characters are the exception, they are open about loving more than one person and do not get jealous the way monogamous characters do.

sections.heading: Cooling off, breakups, and winning someone back
body: Relationships need tending. If you ignore someone for a long stretch, their warm feelings slowly cool and a little Tension creeps in. The more committed you are, the shorter the leash: a casual connection can coast for about two weeks before it starts to drift, but a live-in partner begins to feel neglected after only a few days, and drifts faster once it does.

Commitment is not a finish line; a committed relationship has to be kept healthy. If warmth craters or Tension stays high, from bad dates, a hurtful ask, or prolonged neglect, it goes "on the rocks." That is a warning, and you will get a "we need to talk" text. You have a few days to turn it around with a good date before it ends. A single catastrophic date can break things off immediately, with no warning.

A breakup is not always forever. After a cooling-off period you can win someone back by staying in touch and rebuilding warmth, and they will cautiously give it another go, though each breakup leaves a scar that makes the relationship a little more fragile the next time. Uncommitted relationships never break up outright; they just cool off if you neglect them.

sections.heading: Attraction and intimacy
body: Romance only blooms where there is mutual attraction. If you have set an orientation and a character's orientation clearly rules you out, the bond can still grow into a friendly acquaintance, but it will not cross into the romantic stages, so there is no dating, milestones, jealousy, or intimacy. This is strictly opt-in: if you leave your orientation unset, or either of you is bisexual, non-binary, or unspecified, nothing is gated. When an incompatible pairing levels off, the character may warmly and honestly share their orientation and decline romance.

If you have turned on adult content in Settings (and are using a model that allows it), intimacy is still earned, not automatic. It only becomes possible once you are genuinely close, at the close stage or warmer, and things are calm between you, so a recent fight keeps it off the table until you have smoothed things over. Whatever happens always stays within the character's stated boundaries.

phone.label: Your phone
phone.title: Your phone
phone.overview: Your phone is one tab on the bottom bar, and opening it drops you onto a home screen of apps — like a real phone, complete with the in-world day and time, a warm greeting that shifts with the hour, and a "battery" that quietly tracks how much energy you have left today. Each app is its own little tool: some keep you close to people, others run your life and your money. A few of the money apps only appear if the world you're playing was built to include them.

sections.heading: How the home screen works
body: At the top, the phone always shows the current in-world day and time of day, plus a battery that fills green when you're rested and drains toward red as you spend energy across the day. The greeting under the clock changes with the time — morning, afternoon, evening, or night.

Apps sit in a grid, with four kept in the dock along the bottom: Messages, Mail, Moments, and Settings. A small number badge on an app means something is waiting for you — unread texts, unread mail, or new posts on Faces. Tap any app to open it; the round button below the screen takes you back home.
Three of the money apps — Property, Market, and Casino — only show up when the world you're in was set up to use them. In a world that doesn't, they simply aren't on your home screen.

sections.heading: Staying close — the people apps
body: These are the apps for your relationships: texting, keepsakes, the town feed, and the map of who knows whom. Texting is completely free and never costs you energy, so reach out as often as you like.
bullets: Messages — Text anyone you've been on a date with, any time, for free; it never costs energy. You can attach a photo or a gift to a message, and accept gifts a character sends you. If someone is unavailable that day, a small moon appears next to their name and you'll have to catch them another time.
Mail — Your in-world inbox of letters and notices from companies and strangers as the days pass. The people you date never email you — keep romance in Messages. Tap a letter to read it.
Faces — A town feed where you and the locals post short updates. You can write your own post (up to 500 characters), react six different ways, and leave comments on what other characters share.
Moments — A scrapbook of your story with one person: milestones, dates, and other keepsakes, each one a little snapshot. Pick a character at the top to flip through your memories together.

Constellation — A star-map of every relationship in the world, with you at the warm center and threads out to everyone you've met. Switch to the list to search and filter by the kind of connection, and tap anyone to open their full profile — their circle, their recent life, and what they've heard about you through the grapevine.
Endings — A gallery of keepsake epilogues for relationships you've seen all the way through. It stays empty until you complete a character's story.
Almanac — A calendar of every day you've lived, with its weather, what happened, and a recap of the day. Tap a past day to read its full story. Days you haven't reached yet are locked.
Weather — Today's sky, a week-ahead forecast, and how each character feels about the weather — some love a certain kind of day, some can't stand it.

sections.heading: Running your life — the activity and money apps
body: These apps are how you spend your days and fill your purse. Together and Work both draw on your daily energy, and neither can be used while you're in the middle of a date.
The three wealth apps — Property, Market, and Casino — only appear in worlds that enable them, and they all spend and earn the same money you carry everywhere.
bullets: Together — Spend low-key time with someone through small outings, like a coffee or a walk. Pick a person and a way to hang out, and it nudges where you stand with them. It costs energy and is paused while you're on a date.

Work — Take shifts for money. Some are simple jobs (each costs a set amount of energy, shown as a minus number), and some are graded skill shifts you actually play through. Your career skills level up the more you work them, and higher levels pay more. Keep an eye on your energy readout — you can't work with none left, or while you're on a date.

Market — A stock market that reacts to the world around you. Buy and sell shares, read the market news, and track your portfolio's value and your cash. Some companies pay dividends, which are the only truly hands-off income in the game.

Property — Browse places to rent or buy. Renting means keeping up with the rent — if it falls overdue, you'll get landlord notices in Messages — while buying makes a place yours outright. Some places give a small relationship boost when you bring a date there.

Casino — The House of Fortune, with slots, blackjack, roulette, and video poker. You wager your real in-game money, and a daily wager limit (shown as a ribbon at the top) keeps any single day in check. The house always keeps an edge, so play for fun.
Shop — Buy gifts and keepsakes; your money and your bag are your own in each world.
Games — The arcade of mini-games you can play just for fun.
Bag — Your inventory of items you're carrying, including gifts you can send.
Settings — The phone's own settings: set a custom wallpaper, switch between Play and Creator mode, and (carefully) reset your progress.

life-money.label: Energy & money
life-money.title: Energy, money & activities
life-money.overview: Each in-world day gives you a small pool of energy to spend on real meetings -- dates, casual time together, and minigames. Texting and calling are always free. You start with an empty wallet and no allowance, so spending money comes from working jobs and playing the money-paying minigames; you spend it in the Shop, mostly on gifts. Where a world turns them on, you can also grow -- or gamble away -- your money through the stock market, property, and the casino.

sections.heading: Your daily energy
body: Every day starts with a small pool of energy: 3 actions on a normal day, and one extra on weekends, for 4. Anything that's a real, in-person meeting spends energy. A date, an afternoon spent together with someone, and a minigame each cost one action. A heavier job can cost more.

Texting and calling people on your phone is always free -- it never touches your energy, so you can keep a conversation going as long as you like. When your energy runs out for the day, you can't start another date or work shift; tap Sleep to move to the next day, which fully refills your energy.
Because energy is the real limit on your day, spend it with intent. A heavy outdoor work shift, for instance, uses two of your three weekday actions, leaving room for just one more thing before bed.
bullets: A date, time spent together, or a minigame: 1 action each.
The heavy outdoor day-labor shift: 2 actions.
Texting and calling: free and unlimited.
Sleep to begin a new day and refill your energy (3 on weekdays, 4 on weekends).

sections.heading: Earning money
body: You start with nothing, and there's no daily allowance -- money comes from work. Open the work options and pick the shift that fits your day. The more you repeat a kind of work, the better you get at it: your skill in that trade levels up and slowly raises its pay. No single shift can ever pay a runaway amount, so steady work is the honest road to a full wallet.

Two jobs are played as quick skill games -- the Woodlot (felling logs) and the Copy Desk (transcribing the day's news). They pay you purely for how well you play, up to a solid flat payout, and a good run there is as good as it gets for one shift.
Beyond work, the only money that arrives on its own is dividends from any stocks you're holding, in worlds that have a stock market. Nothing pays you just for sleeping.
bullets: Work a shift -- steady, dependable pay with no surprises; builds your Service skill.
Hustle odd jobs -- the same pay on average but a wide swing, great on a good day and thin on a bad one; builds your Hustle skill.
Day-labor outdoors -- the biggest single payday, but it takes two actions and the weather sets the price (storms pay best, fair days least); unlocks once you've built up your Craft skill.
The Woodlot and the Copy Desk -- minigame jobs that pay you for how well you play.

sections.heading: Spending money: the Shop, your Bag, and gifts
body: The Shop is where your earnings go -- gifts, keepsakes, clothing, books, and the occasional consumable. Anything you buy lands in your inventory (your Bag) at the listed price; nothing in the Shop ever leaves you with more money than you started with.

Gifts are how money turns into affection. You can hand someone a gift on a date or attach one to a text. They'll react in their own voice -- a spoken line, a feeling, sometimes a lasting keepsake memory -- and the gesture nudges your relationship. A single gift is only ever a small nudge (no more than a few points on any one feeling), and gifts can't be spammed: the first one you give someone in a day lands in full, a second the same day lands at about half, and a third or more that day does nothing. Thoughtful timing beats a pile of presents.
Items meant for your own use (consumables) stay in your Bag for you. Only true gifts, clothing, books, and keepsakes can actually be handed to someone.

sections.heading: Growing your money: market, property, and the casino
body: Some worlds switch on extra wealth systems. They're optional, and a world that leaves them off simply won't show them.

The stock market lets you buy and sell shares in the world's fictional companies. Prices drift from day to day and can react to big relationship moments in your world, so a portfolio is a gamble, not a guarantee. Some companies pay a small dividend each day on shares you've held at least a full day -- the one bit of money that arrives without you working for it.

Property lets you lease a place -- paying rent on a set schedule, whether daily, weekly, or monthly -- or buy one outright. Owning or leasing a place lets you take dates there, and a date at a place you own gives a warmer boost than one you've only rented for the night. Watch your rent: miss a payment and you'll get a landlord warning and three days' grace before you're evicted. Selling a place you own simply refunds what you paid for it.

The casino, where enabled, offers slots, blackjack, roulette, and video poker. The house always has an edge, so treat it as risk and flavor, never an income plan. Each bet has a minimum (5) and a cap, and there's a limit on how much you can wager in a single day, so a bad streak can't wipe you out in one sitting.

building.label: Building worlds
building.title: Building your world
building.overview: Heartmorrow has two modes. In Play mode you simply live in a world: date, text, work, and watch relationships grow. Creator mode unlocks the building tools on top of everything you can already do, so you can author your own people, places, and whole worlds. This page covers creating characters and worlds, turning optional systems on or off, the built-in AI helpers, sharing your creations as files, the adult-content toggle, and what a progress reset does (and doesn't) erase.

sections.heading: Play mode vs. Creator mode
body: Heartmorrow runs in one of two modes, and you can switch between them at any time. Play mode keeps the building and editing tools out of the way so you can just enjoy the world. Creator mode adds those tools on top of everything Play mode already does.

You set the mode under the "Mode" heading on the main Settings page (reachable from the world list via "Switch world" → "Settings," or the Settings tab once you're in a world). You can also flip it from your phone's Settings app, under "Mode." Your choice is a personal preference saved on this device, not part of any world's save.

Turning on Creator mode reveals two extra spots in the main navigation: a "World" tab (a behind-the-scenes look at your world's cast and simulation) and a "Debug" area for diagnostics. It also unlocks editing controls throughout the app — for example, a "Generate items" button appears in the Shop, and a delete button appears on each card in the world list.
bullets: Play mode: date and live in the world, with the editing tools tucked away.
Creator mode: everything in Play mode, plus the character and world editors, the World and Debug tabs, and in-place generate, edit, and delete controls.
Switch it under "Mode" in Settings, or in the phone's Settings app. It's a per-device preference, not part of your save.

sections.heading: Creating and editing characters
body: The character workbench is organized into five tabs: Identity, Personality, Profile, Relationships, and World. A new character starts at age 18 (the minimum the game allows) and, unless you change it, belongs to the world you're currently playing.

Identity holds the basics: name, age, pronouns, gender, sexuality, which world they live in, their relationship style (Monogamous or Polyamorous), and a short description. Personality is where the writing lives — their personality, how they speak, what they're looking for in a relationship, plus tag lists for their likes, dislikes, goals, and boundaries. There's also a private "creator notes" field that the character itself never sees.

One trait worth understanding is Guardedness, a 0–100 slider running from "an open book" to "walled off." It controls how slowly someone warms up to you on a date: guarded people open up more slowly and are harder to win over (and just as easy to lose). New characters start mildly reserved, and the label under the slider updates as you drag it.

The Profile tab fleshes out their appearance, texting style, online persona, love language, and intimate-chemistry details. The Relationships tab is where you wire social connections to other people in the same world — Friend, Rival, Ex, Family, Partner, Crush, Roommate, Coworker, Classmate, Neighbor, Mentor, or Mentee. Connections never cross worlds, so build a world's cast first, then link them together. (Their evolving relationship stats with you and their memories also live on this tab, once the character has been saved.)
bullets: Six dating stats — Charm, Empathy, Humor, Confidence, Intellect, and Style — each run 0–100 and default to 50. They color how a character talks, shape the "Together" activities, and decide their favorite minigame (their highest stat picks it).
Likes, dislikes, goals, and boundaries are free-text tags you add one at a time.
Social connections only link people who share a world — so create the cast first, then connect them.

sections.heading: Generating characters from a description or image
body: You don't have to fill in every field by hand. The character workbench has a "Generate" button that drafts a whole character from a portrait image, a block of reference text, or both. Pick a portrait, paste or upload some descriptive text (up to 40,000 characters; an uploaded file is read as plain text), and let it draft the identity, personality, traits, profile, and starting dating stats.

Generating fills the written fields but keeps your chosen portrait, expression images, and world, and it never touches your private creator notes. If you've already written real content, you'll get a heads-up before it's overwritten.

There are also smaller, focused helpers. On the Profile section, a "Generate from description" button fills in the appearance, texting, and chemistry details from what you've already written, and the dating-stats section has its own button to suggest a stat spread. Both of those need a name filled in first. Everything generated arrives as a draft you can review and edit before saving, and all of it relies on the AI model you've connected in Settings.

sections.heading: Building worlds, locations, and optional systems
body: The world workbench has a sidebar listing your worlds (with a "New world" button) and a detail pane for the one you've selected. Here you set the world's name, summary, tone, lore, in-fiction rules, and global notes. You can also add World Notes — scoped reference entries (global, location, faction, lore, rule, character, or misc) that are fed to the AI as background.

Locations are the venues your dates happen in. Each has a name, description, tags, an indoor/outdoor setting, an optional photo, and a "cost to date there" price tier. You can add them by hand or use "Generate with AI," which drafts a batch (1 to 8 at a time) from your world's lore plus a free-form prompt. Generated locations drop straight into the editor for you to review and tweak — they're only kept once you click "Save world."

Each world can turn three optional systems on or off independently, under "Game features": Property (rent or buy places), the Stock Market, and the Casino. All three are off by default, and the setting is enforced per world — if a world has a feature off, those screens simply aren't available there. When you enable the Casino, you can also set its betting limits.

Worlds are also where shop stock comes from: open the Shop in Creator mode and use "Generate items" to draft themed gifts and goods (your world's setting is woven in automatically), then keep the ones you like.
bullets: Property, Stock Market, and Casino are per-world toggles, all off by default.
Casino limits you can set: a maximum single bet (up to 1,000) and a daily wager cap (up to 10,000), with a minimum bet of 5.
A location's price tier sets how much a date there costs; the "Free" tier costs nothing.
Deleting a world offers an optional checkbox to also delete its characters — leave it unchecked to keep the cast.

sections.heading: Sharing: export and import
body: Your creations can be packaged into share files and handed to other players. There are three kinds: one or more characters as a .hmchr file, a whole world as a .hmwrld file, and a mixed bundle of worlds and loose characters as a .hmpack file. Share files carry definitions and images only — never your save. No relationships, money, memories, messages, or day history travels with them.

To export, go to the world list, choose "Export worlds," pick what to share, then set a title and an optional note in the preview dialog. When you export a world, you can choose whether its cast of characters comes along.

To import, use the "Import file" button on the world list. You'll see a read-only preview of exactly what's inside — the worlds, the people, and the image counts — before anything is added. Importing is always additive: it creates fresh copies and never overwrites or deletes what you already have. When a file contains both worlds and people, you can choose to take just the worlds or bring the cast too.

sections.heading: Adult content, and resetting vs. keeping your work
body: The adult-content toggle lives in Settings and is off by default. Turning it on requires confirming a short acknowledgment, including that you're of age. With it on, intimate scenes can unfold on dates once a relationship is genuinely close and not too tense — the game still gates intimacy by how warm and settled the bond is, so it never happens out of nowhere. With it off, characters keep things tasteful and will set a boundary if you push too far too soon. There's also an opt-in "tragic outcomes" sub-toggle (available only once adult content is on) that allows darker story endings; it shows support resources before you enable it.
Keep in mind that adult content is a global preference, and the kind of writing you actually get also depends on the AI model you've connected.

Finally, the "Danger zone" in your phone's Settings app has a reset that wipes your playthrough — relationships, memories, conversations, texts, emails, and inventory — and sends you back to Day 1 with your starting money. Crucially, it keeps everything you authored: your worlds, characters, world notes, shop items, and uploaded images all survive. A reset is a clean slate for playing, not for building.

tips.label: Tips & help
tips.title: Tips & troubleshooting
tips.overview: A few habits make Heartmorrow click, and most snags have a quick explanation. Here are the things worth knowing and the answers to the questions that come up most.

sections.heading: Get the most out of the AI
body: Every line of dialogue and every judgement comes from the language model you connect in Settings, so the model matters more than anything else. A stronger, more capable model writes characters who feel more like themselves and reads your dates more sharply — it is better at telling real warmth from filler and at noticing when you hit a like, a dislike, or a boundary.

If dates ever feel too easy, too generous, or a little off, connecting a better model is the most direct fix. A local model keeps everything on your own machine for free; a hosted model usually reads nuance more reliably. Adult content, if you turn it on, also depends on the model being willing to write it.

sections.heading: Habits that pay off
bullets: Put real effort into what you say. Low-effort, going-nowhere messages quietly cool a date — there is no coasting.
Learn the person. Their likes, dislikes, and boundaries shape how the night is judged, so play to what they care about and steer clear of what they don't.
Time your gifts. A gift is a small, thoughtful nudge, not a shortcut — the first one a day lands in full, a second lands at about half, and more do nothing.
Tend your commitments. The closer you are, the sooner neglect stings — a live-in partner starts to feel ignored after only a few days.
Stay in touch. Texting and calling are free and never cost energy, so keep the conversation going between dates.

sections.heading: Common questions
bullets: Dates feel too easy, or the scoring seems generous — the judging is only as discerning as the model reading it. Connect a more capable model in Settings.
Nobody texts me — you only hear from people you've already been on a date with. Someone you dated and then neglected may send a wistful message; people you've never met never text.
I can't sleep or end the day — you have a date in progress. Finish it with "End & evaluate," or leave it, and the day button unlocks.
I'm out of money — there's no allowance. Open the Work app and take a shift; that's how you earn.
An app is missing from my phone — Property, Market, and Casino only appear in worlds that switch them on (a per-world setting in Creator mode).
They keep walking out on me — walkouts happen only for hostile or cruel behavior, or coming on strong with someone you're not close to yet. Ease off and let the vibe recover.
My ask to define the relationship failed — you need enough warmth and good timing. After any ask they want a few days before you bring it up again, so try again after a good date.
A character treats an old plan as if it is still on — texts can go stale; the game flags when a plan is days old, but smaller models occasionally miss it.

sections.heading: Starting over
body: Want a clean slate? The Danger zone in your phone's Settings app resets your playthrough — relationships, memories, conversations, texts, emails, and inventory all clear, and you return to Day 1 with your starting money. Everything you authored survives: your worlds, characters, notes, shop items, and images are all kept. A reset is a fresh start for playing, not for building.
